strInstructions: Cook the rice with butterfly pea flower water, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, and salt until fragrant and lightly blue. Prepare the herb salad by finely slicing cucumber, long beans, bean sprouts, cabbage, daun kesum, mint, torch ginger flower, and other fresh herbs. Prepare the fish coconut mixture by combining flaked cooked fish with toasted grated coconut, shallots, lemongrass, ginger, and seasoning. Serve the blue rice with the herb salad, coconut fish mixture, salted egg, crackers, sambal, and grilled or fried fish.

Soak glutinous rice and long grain rice separately overnight, then drain and mix. Steam the rice until partly cooked. Mix coconut milk with salt, shallots, ginger, and fenugreek, then fold it into the rice. Steam again until the rice is fully cooked and fragrant. Serve with gulai ikan tongkol, pickled vegetables, and hard-boiled eggs.
searchAliases: malaysian coconut rice with fish curry

strMeal: Malaysian Coconut Chicken Curry Nasi Kandar
strInstructions: Blend or pound onion, garlic, ginger, and chilies into a paste. Heat oil in a pan and fry the spice paste with curry leaves and curry powder until fragrant. Add chicken pieces and coat them well in the paste. Pour in coconut milk and water or stock, then simmer until the chicken is cooked through and the sauce thickens. Season with salt, sugar, and lime or tamarind if needed. Serve the curry over steamed rice with cucumber, boiled egg, and extra curry sauce.
searchAliases: malaysian coconut chicken curry nasi kandar

strInstructions: Rinse the basmati rice until the water runs clear, then soak for 30 minutes and drain. Heat ghee or butter in a pan and fry the cinnamon stick, cardamom pods, cloves, and star anise until fragrant. Add onion paste, garlic paste, and ginger paste, then sauté until aromatic. Add tomato puree, sliced tomatoes, pandan leaves, and evaporated milk, then cook until the tomato mixture thickens slightly. Transfer the drained rice to a rice cooker, add the tomato mixture, water, salt, raisins, and cashews. Cook on the white rice setting, rest for 10 minutes, then fluff. Garnish with fried shallots and coriander leaves.

Coat the chicken with turmeric and salt, then brown or half-fry the pieces briefly and set aside. Blend dried chilies, onion, garlic, ginger, and belacan or shrimp paste into a smooth spice paste. Heat oil and sauté the spice paste with lemongrass until fragrant and the oil separates. Add tomato paste, tomato ketchup, water, sugar, and seasoning. Return the chicken to the pan and simmer until fully cooked and the sauce reduces into a thick red gravy.

Prepare the spice paste by blending shallots, garlic, and ginger until smooth. Blend lemongrass separately with a little water so it stays slightly fibrous. Coarsely grind coriander seeds, fennel seeds, and cumin seeds. In a large bowl, combine chicken with the spice paste, lemongrass, curry powder, chili powder, turmeric powder, ground spices, rice flour, salt, and curry leaves. Marinate for at least 1 hour or overnight. Heat oil to 170 to 180°C. Fry the chicken in batches until golden brown, crispy, and fully cooked. Fry the remaining marinade bits until crisp and sprinkle them over the chicken.
strMeasure6: 1 tbsp coarsely ground or 2 tsp ground coriander
strMeasure7: 1/2 tbsp coarsely ground or 1 tsp ground fennel
strMeasure8: 1/2 tbsp coarsely ground or 1 tsp ground cumin
strMeasure16: 1/4 cup or just enough for blending

strInstructions: Soak the dried chilies until softened, then blend them with shallots, garlic, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, candlenuts, and belacan into a smooth spice paste. Heat oil in a pot and fry the paste until fragrant and the oil separates. Add the chicken pieces and coat them well in the paste. Add coconut milk, water, kaffir lime leaves, and salt. Simmer gently until the chicken is tender and the curry becomes thick. Finish with crispy fried shallots and serve with rice.

Marinate the chicken pieces with turmeric powder, chili powder, curry powder, and salt. Heat oil in a pan and sauté onions, garlic, ginger, curry leaves, cinnamon, cloves, star anise, and cardamom until fragrant. Add the marinated chicken and stir well. Cook uncovered, stirring occasionally, until the chicken releases its juices and begins to dry out. Add tomatoes or a little water only if needed. Continue cooking until the chicken is coated in a dark, dry masala and the oil separates.

Marinate the chicken with curry powder, turmeric, salt, pepper, and cornstarch. Soak dried shrimp in hot water until softened, then chop finely. Heat oil in a wok and fry onion, garlic, chilies, lemongrass, dried shrimp, curry leaves, and kaffir lime leaves until aromatic. Add the chicken and stir-fry until nearly cooked. Mix oyster sauce, soy sauce, dark soy sauce, curry powder, sugar, and a little water, then pour into the wok. Toss until the chicken is cooked through and coated in a dry, fragrant sauce.

Mix chicken pieces with soy sauce, then coat with cornstarch. Heat oil and fry the chicken until golden and crisp, then drain and set aside. In a wok, combine water, Marmite, honey, maltose, and Shaoxing wine. Cook until the sauce becomes glossy and slightly thick. Add the fried chicken and toss quickly until evenly coated. Serve immediately.

Blend dried chilies, shallots, garlic, ginger, turmeric, and belacan into a spice paste. Heat oil and fry the paste until fragrant and the oil separates. Add tamarind juice, water, lemongrass, daun kesum, and tomatoes, then simmer until aromatic. Add fish pieces and okra. Simmer gently until the fish is cooked. Season with salt and sugar, then serve hot with rice.

Blend dried chilies, shallots, garlic, and belacan into a paste. Fry the paste in oil until fragrant and the oil separates. Add tamarind juice, sugar, and salt, then simmer until the sambal thickens. Add prawns and cook just until they turn pink and are coated in the sambal. Serve hot with rice.

Blend the dried chilies, shallots, garlic, candlenuts, lemongrass, turmeric, galangal, and belacan into a smooth spice paste. Heat oil in a wok or pan and sauté the paste until it becomes fragrant and drier. Transfer the sautéed paste to a cooking pot, then add water and pineapple pieces. Simmer for about 15 minutes until the pineapple releases its sweetness into the gravy. Pour in the thick coconut milk and bring the curry to a gentle boil for 2 to 3 minutes. Add the prawns and cook just until the prawns are cooked through. Turn off the heat and serve hot with steamed rice.
strMeasure1: 15-20 pieces, seeds discarded and soaked in hot water for 20 mins

strInstructions: Blend shallots, lemongrass, candlenuts, kaffir lime leaves, garlic, ginger, galangal, chilies, turmeric, oil, tamarind, salt, and brown sugar into a spice paste. Coat the fish with the paste and let it absorb the flavor. Line a grill pan or grill with banana leaves and brush with coconut oil. Grill the fish until cooked through and slightly charred. Serve hot with extra lime or sambal.

Blend lemongrass, shallots, garlic, turmeric, chilies, and aromatics into a paste. Mix the paste with fish, coconut milk, eggs, salt, and sliced daun kaduk until well combined. Spoon the mixture onto banana leaves or into a heatproof dish. Steam or grill until the custard is set and fragrant. Serve warm as a snack or side dish.

Prepare the sauce by mixing soy sauce, dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, and chili paste. Heat a wok until very hot. Fry garlic in oil, then add prawns and Chinese sausage. Add noodles and sauce, tossing quickly over high heat. Push aside, crack in the egg, then add bean sprouts and chives. Stir-fry until smoky and glossy, then serve immediately.

Heat oil in a wok and fry garlic until fragrant. Add pork and stir-fry briefly. Mix oyster sauce, soy sauce, water, and dark soy sauce, then add the sauce to the wok. Add hokkien noodles and braise until the noodles absorb the gravy. Add prawns and choy sum near the end. Finish with crispy pork lard and serve hot.

Blend aromatics and spices into a curry paste. Fry the paste until fragrant, then add chicken broth, coconut milk, and seasoning. Simmer until the broth becomes rich and aromatic. Cook yellow noodles and rice vermicelli separately. Place noodles in bowls and top with curry broth, chicken, tofu puffs, bean sprouts, and herbs. Serve with chili paste.

Prepare the chili paste by frying dried chilies, garlic, dried shrimp, and seasonings until aromatic, then grind or blend into a coarse chili oil. Cook the noodles according to package instructions or prepare fresh pan mee noodles. Stir-fry the minced pork with garlic, soy sauce, oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, and pepper until savory and cooked through. Fry anchovies until crisp. To assemble, place noodles in a bowl with dark sauce or seasoning sauce, then top with minced pork, crispy anchovies, poached egg, fried shallots, scallions, and chili paste. Mix well before eating.

Marinate chicken pieces with soy sauce, oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, sesame oil, Shaoxing wine, and cornstarch. Rinse rice and cook it in a claypot with water or chicken stock. When the rice is partly cooked, add the marinated chicken, Chinese sausage, mushrooms, and ginger. Cover and cook until the rice is tender and the chicken is fully cooked. Garnish with scallions and serve hot.

Poach chicken gently with ginger, scallions, and salt until just cooked. Chill briefly to keep the skin firm. Use chicken fat to fry garlic, ginger, and rice, then cook the rice with chicken broth and pandan leaves. Make chili sauce with chilies, garlic, ginger, lime juice, and chicken broth. Serve sliced chicken with fragrant rice, cucumber, soup, and sauces.

Bring water to a boil in a large pot. Add rinsed bak kut teh herbs, dong gui if using, soaked shiitake mushrooms, garlic, pork ribs, dark soy sauce, rock sugar, and salt. Simmer gently until the pork ribs are tender and the broth is deeply flavored. Skim off excess foam and oil as needed. Add tofu puffs near the end and simmer until heated through. Serve hot with steamed rice, fried dough sticks, and sliced chilies in soy sauce.
strIngredient7: Baby Back Ribs or Pork Ribs

strInstructions: Prepare the stuffing by mixing ground pork, fish paste, salted fish or fish sauce, cornstarch, salt, sugar, and white pepper until sticky. Cut slits or pockets into tofu, bitter gourd, okra, eggplant, mushrooms, and large chilies. Stuff each piece with the pork and fish mixture. Steam, boil, or pan-fry the stuffed tofu and vegetables until the filling is cooked through. Serve with soup or a sweet bean sauce and chili sauce.
strMeasure3: 20 g salted fish or 1 tbsp fish sauce

strInstructions: Prepare the filling by stir-frying shredded jicama, carrot, dried shrimp, garlic, and seasoning until the vegetables are soft and flavorful. Drain excess liquid from the filling. Lay a popiah wrapper on a plate and spread hoisin sauce and sambal down the center. Add lettuce, the cooked jicama filling, fried bean curd, prawns, sliced egg omelette, fried shallots, peanuts, and green onions. Roll tightly, slice if desired, and serve fresh.

Prepare the filling by sautéing jicama until softened and flavorful. Prepare egg omelette strips and cucumber strips for topping. If making the shells from scratch, prepare the batter, heat the pie tee mold in oil, dip the mold into the batter, then deep-fry until crisp. Fill each shell with the cooked jicama, omelette strips, cucumber, and chili sauce. Serve immediately so the shells remain crispy.

Cut the cucumber, carrot, cabbage, long beans, and pineapple into bite-sized pieces. Blanch firmer vegetables briefly, then drain well. Blend chilies, shallots, garlic, turmeric, and other aromatics into a spice paste. Fry the paste until fragrant, then add vinegar, sugar, and salt to make a sweet-sour pickling sauce. Toss in the vegetables, crushed peanuts, and sesame seeds. Let the acar cool and rest before serving.

Soak rice vermicelli until soft, then blanch and drain. Prepare kaffir lime leaves, lemongrass, shallots, garlic, and torch ginger flower, then place them in a large mixing bowl. Toast grated coconut until browned and add it to the bowl. Soak dried shrimp, then pound or process coarsely and toast until fragrant. Add cooked prawns, herbs, and the vermicelli. Season with sambal belacan, calamansi or lime juice, fish sauce, and sugar. Toss until the noodles are evenly coated and serve at room temperature.

Combine flour, egg, coconut milk, water, turmeric powder, and salt into a smooth batter. Strain to remove lumps. Transfer the batter into a roti jala mold, squeeze bottle, or piping bag. Heat a nonstick pan and drizzle the batter in a lacy net pattern. Cook briefly until set but still soft. Fold or roll the crepe and repeat with the remaining batter. Serve with curry.

Blend shallots, garlic, ginger, galangal, lemongrass, and spices into a paste. Fry the paste until fragrant, then add chicken, water, and soup spices. Simmer until the chicken is cooked and the broth is aromatic. Remove and shred the chicken. Prepare noodles or rice cakes separately. Serve the broth with noodles, shredded chicken, bean sprouts, herbs, fried shallots, and sambal cili kicap.

Blanch the lamb or mutton pieces, then rinse and drain. Heat oil in a large pot and sauté onion, garlic, ginger, cinnamon, star anise, cardamom, cloves, and spice paste until fragrant. Add the lamb and water, then simmer until the meat begins to soften. Add potatoes, carrots, celery, and tomatoes. Continue simmering until the lamb is tender and the broth is full-bodied. Season with salt and pepper, then serve with fried shallots, cilantro, lime, and bread.

Rinse the rice and set aside. Heat oil in a pot and sauté onion, garlic, ginger, cinnamon, star anise, cardamom, cloves, and ground spices until fragrant. Add minced beef or diced beef and cook until browned. Add rice and water or stock, then simmer until the rice breaks down into porridge. Stir often to prevent sticking. Add coconut milk if using and season with salt. Garnish with fried shallots, spring onions, and herbs before serving.

Coat the chicken pieces with turmeric powder and salt, then marinate for 30 minutes or overnight for better flavor. Blend the cili padi, onion, garlic, lemongrass, and ginger into a paste. Heat oil in a pot over medium heat, then add the marinated chicken and pan-sear on both sides. Add the blended paste and stir until fragrant and combined with the chicken. Pour in water and season with salt, then bring to a boil and let the chicken braise until fully cooked. Lower the heat and pour in coconut milk or coconut cream. Tear in the makrut lime leaves and stir gently to combine. Once the liquid comes back to a gentle boil, turn off the heat and squeeze in lime juice. Serve immediately with rice.
strMeasure3: 1 tsp for marinade plus more to taste

strInstructions: Blend turmeric, ginger, bird's eye chilies, red chilies, shallots, garlic, lemongrass, and water into a spice paste. Heat oil in a pot and sauté the spice paste until fragrant. Add sliced daging salai and cook until the beef is coated with the paste. Add water, bird's eye chilies, and asam keping, then simmer until the beef is heated through and the flavors combine. Add evaporated milk or coconut milk and turmeric leaf, then bring to a gentle simmer. Season with chicken stock powder or salt and serve immediately with rice.
strIngredient3: Evaporated Filled Milk or Coconut Milk
strMeasure4: 200 ml plus 100 ml for spice paste

strInstructions: Blend onions, garlic, and ginger. Sauté the paste with cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, star anise, and kurma powder until fragrant. Add beef and coat in the spices. Add water and simmer until tender. Add coconut milk, potatoes, carrots, and seasoning, then continue cooking until the gravy thickens.

Marinate the beef with kicap manis, dark soy sauce, ground coriander, ground cumin, and salt. Blend onion, garlic, ginger, dried chilies, and water into a smooth spice paste. Heat oil in a pot and sauté cinnamon, star anise, cardamom, and the blended paste until fragrant and the oil begins to separate. Add the marinated beef and stir until well coated. Pour in enough water to cover the beef, then simmer until the meat is tender. Continue cooking until the sauce reduces into a dark, glossy gravy. Adjust seasoning and serve with rice.
strMeasure12: 1/4 cup for blending plus more for simmering

strInstructions: Blend shallots, garlic, ginger, lemongrass, galangal, candlenuts, chilies, and belacan. Fry the paste with curry powder until the oil separates. Add chicken and potatoes and coat well. Add coconut milk, water, and kaffir lime leaves. Simmer until the chicken is tender and the potatoes are soft. Season with salt and sugar.

Blend shallots and garlic into a paste. Fry the paste until fragrant, then add fermented soybean paste and cook until aromatic. Add chicken and potatoes, then stir to coat. Add water, dark soy sauce, sugar, and seasoning. Simmer until the chicken is tender, potatoes are soft, and the sauce is savory and slightly sweet.

Make the coconut filling by cooking gula Melaka, sugar, pandan leaf, and water until the sugar dissolves. Add grated coconut and cook until moist and fragrant, then stir in cornstarch to bind the filling. For the crepes, mix flour, egg, coconut milk, pandan juice, salt, and water into a smooth batter. Strain if needed. Cook thin green crepes in a lightly greased nonstick pan. Place coconut filling on each crepe, fold in the sides, and roll tightly.
strMeasure4: 50 ml for filling plus as needed for batter

strInstructions: Mix glutinous rice flour with pandan juice to form a soft dough. Cut palm sugar into small cubes. Wrap each palm sugar cube with dough and roll into balls. Boil until the balls float. Drain and roll in grated coconut mixed with salt. Serve warm so the palm sugar center stays molten.

Mix rice flour, tapioca flour, sugar, coconut milk, water, and salt into a smooth batter. Divide the batter into separate bowls and color each portion. Grease a steaming tray and steam one thin layer until set before adding the next layer. Stir the batter before pouring each layer because the flour settles at the bottom. Continue layering and steaming until all batter is used. Cool completely before loosening and slicing.

Bring water and palm sugar to a boil with pandan leaves, stirring until dissolved. Let the syrup cool slightly, then strain. Mix rice flour, tapioca flour, and lye water with the palm sugar syrup until smooth. Grease small cups or molds and preheat them in the steamer. Pour in the batter and steam until firm and translucent. Cool completely, remove from molds, and coat with grated coconut mixed with salt.

Blend pandan leaves with water and strain to make pandan juice. For the green layer, mix mung bean starch, tapioca starch, rice flour, sugar, pandan juice, water, and alkaline water until smooth, then cook gently until slightly thickened. Pour into a greased tray and steam until set. For the white coconut layer, mix rice flour, tapioca flour, coconut milk, water, and salt until smooth. Pour over the green layer and steam again until firm. Cool completely before slicing.
strMeasure2: 300 ml for pandan juice plus more for coconut layer

strInstructions: Prepare butterfly pea flower juice and let it cool. Soak part of the glutinous rice in the blue flower juice and the rest in plain water for at least 4 hours or overnight. Drain the rice. Mix coconut milk with water, salt, and sugar. Steam the rice with pandan leaves, then mix with the coconut milk mixture and steam again until tender. Lightly combine the blue and white rice to create a marbled pattern. Press firmly into a lined tray, cool, cut into squares, and serve with kaya jam.
strMeasure2: for soaking part of the rice
strMeasure3: for soaking plus for coconut mixture

strInstructions: Steam or boil sweet potatoes, taro, and yam until tender. Prepare sago pearls separately until translucent. Simmer coconut milk with pandan leaves, sugar, and salt. Add the cooked root vegetables and sago, then warm through gently without boiling hard. Serve warm or chilled.

Prepare the green jelly by cooking rice flour or mung bean starch with pandan juice until thick and glossy. Press the hot mixture through a cendol mold or colander into cold water to form green strands. Make the palm sugar syrup by simmering palm sugar with water until dissolved and slightly thickened. To serve, place shaved ice in a bowl or glass, add cendol strands, coconut milk, palm sugar syrup, and red beans if using.
strIngredient1: Rice Flour or Mung Bean Starch

strInstructions: Prepare the palm sugar syrup by simmering gula Melaka with water until dissolved and slightly thickened. Prepare the toppings such as sweet red beans, grass jelly, creamed corn, palm seeds, and cendol. Place the toppings in serving bowls or glasses. Add shaved ice over the toppings, then drizzle with gula Melaka syrup, evaporated milk, and condensed milk if using. Serve immediately while the ice is cold.

Break Marie biscuits into pieces and set aside. Melt butter or coconut oil in a saucepan over low heat. Add condensed milk, Milo powder, cocoa powder, salt, and vanilla extract, then stir until smooth and thick. Fold in the biscuit pieces until evenly coated. Press the mixture firmly into a lined pan. Chill until set. For a richer finish, pour chocolate ganache over the top and chill again before slicing.

Blend pandan leaves with coconut milk, then strain the pandan-infused coconut milk through a sieve. Beat eggs and sugar in a heatproof bowl until frothy. Slowly mix in the pandan coconut milk. Cook the mixture gently over a double boiler, stirring constantly, until it thickens into a smooth custard-like jam. Strain if needed, cool, and store in jars. Serve with toast or steamed bread.

Toast belacan until fragrant. Pound fresh chilies with the toasted belacan until coarse. Add calamansi juice, sugar, and salt. Pound lightly to combine. Taste and adjust until spicy, salty, sour, and slightly sweet. Serve with ulam, rice, fried fish, or noodles.

Mash ripe bananas in a mixing bowl until soft and slightly chunky. Add flour, sugar, salt, and baking powder, then mix into a thick batter. Heat oil in a pan or wok over medium heat. Drop spoonfuls of batter into the hot oil and fry until golden brown and crisp outside. Drain on paper towels and serve warm as a tea-time snack.
